questions.q: What is the primary advantage of a star schema compared to a normalized relational schema?
options: Simpler queries with fewer joins, better query performance, and easier for business users to understand
Star schemas use less storage space because they eliminate redundancy completely
Star schemas automatically prevent all data quality issues without validation
Normalized schemas cannot support aggregations while star schemas can
questions.explanation: Star schemas denormalize dimensions around a central fact table, making queries simpler (fewer joins) and faster. Analysts can write straightforward group-by queries instead of complex multi-table joins. The trade-off is controlled redundancy in dimensions, which is acceptable because dimension tables are typically much smaller than facts.

questions.q: How does a snowflake schema differ from a star schema?
options: Snowflake further normalizes dimension tables into sub-dimensions, reducing redundancy but increasing join complexity
Snowflake schemas only work with cloud databases while star schemas work anywhere
Snowflake schemas use more joins than star schemas but are faster
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Snowflake schemas normalize dimension tables to remove redundancy. For example, a date dimension might split into separate year/month/day tables. While snowflake saves storage, the extra joins typically make queries slower. Most modern data warehouses prefer star schemas for analytical simplicity.

questions.q: What is the key operational difference between OLAP and OLTP systems?
options: OLAP optimizes for complex analytical queries on large datasets; OLTP optimizes for fast individual transactional operations
OLAP systems are faster than OLTP systems for all query types
OLTP systems are used for data analysis while OLAP systems handle customer transactions
OLAP and OLTP are identical systems with different names
questions.explanation: OLTP (Online Transactional Processing) prioritizes sub-second response times for individual inserts/updates like bank transactions. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) optimizes for scanning millions of rows to answer questions like 'total revenue by region.' They require different indexing and storage strategies.

questions.q: Why are columnar storage formats preferred in data warehouses over row-oriented storage?
options: Columnar storage compresses similar data values efficiently and enables selective column access, improving query performance and reducing I/O
Columnar storage uses less memory than row storage for all operations
Columnar storage automatically eliminates the need for indexes
Row storage cannot support aggregation queries
questions.explanation: Columnar formats like Parquet store all values of a column together, enabling compression algorithms to work better (similar values compress well) and allowing queries to read only needed columns. For analytical queries that touch few columns, columnar reduces I/O by 10-100x compared to row format.

questions.q: In a fact table, what characterizes a measure (metric) versus a dimension?
options: Measures are numeric and aggregate (like sales amount); dimensions are categorical and describe context (like date, region, product)
Measures are categories and dimensions are numbers
All numeric columns in fact tables are dimensions
Measures and dimensions serve the same purpose and the distinction is just terminology
questions.explanation: Fact tables store quantitative measurements that are aggregated in analysis (sum, average, count). Dimensions provide the context for those measurements. A sales fact table might have measure 'amount' and dimensions 'customer', 'product', 'date'. This structure enables dimensional analysis.

questions.q: What problem does a bridge table solve in dimensional modeling?
options: Handling many-to-many relationships between dimensions and facts (e.g., multiple products per order line item)
Storing historical fact data across multiple time periods
Connecting OLTP source systems to the data warehouse
Reducing the number of joins needed in star schema queries
questions.explanation: Bridge tables (also called bridge dimensions) represent many-to-many relationships. For instance, an order line might have multiple associated products, or a promotion might apply to many products. The bridge table has foreign keys to both the fact and dimension tables.

questions.q: How does conformed dimensioning improve consistency across multiple fact tables?
options: Using the same dimension table definition and keys across multiple fact tables ensures consistent analysis across subjects
Conformed dimensions require duplicating dimension data across every fact table
Conformed dimensioning makes star schemas more complex by adding more joins
It prevents fact tables from referencing the same dimension at all
questions.explanation: Conformed dimensions share the same business rules, keys, and attributes across multiple fact tables. When a customer dimension is used in both the sales fact table and the returns fact table, they have one consistent definition of customer. This enables consistent drilling and comparison across subjects.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a factless fact table?
options: Recording events or relationships without numeric measures (e.g., student-course enrollments, coverage relationships)
Temporarily storing data until it can be moved to a regular fact table
A placeholder used when a data warehouse hasn't been designed yet
Tables that only contain dimensions with no connection to facts
questions.explanation: Factless fact tables capture events or relationships. An enrollment fact table might record only student_id and course_id dimensions without measures—counting rows tells you how many enrollments exist. These tables are useful for 'who-what-when' analysis even when no numerical metrics apply.

questions.q: How can a data warehouse handle rapid dimension changes without breaking analytical continuity?
options: Using Slowly Changing Dimension techniques to create version rows or maintain historical attributes while linking facts to correct versions
Deleting old dimension rows so only the current version exists
Creating a new fact table every time a dimension attribute changes
Manually adjusting historical facts to match the new dimension values
questions.explanation: SCDs (Slowly Changing Dimensions) like Type 1, 2, and 3 handle dimension changes. Type 2 adds new rows with validity dates, allowing historical facts to link to the correct dimension version. This preserves analytical accuracy—a sale attributed to a product when it was in category 'Electronics' stays accurate even after recategorization.

questions.q: Why might a company add a data mart as an intermediary layer between the warehouse and business users?
options: Pre-aggregating and denormalizing data for specific business domains improves query performance and simplifies user queries for that subject area
Data marts are cheaper than data warehouses so they replace warehouses entirely
Data marts have no performance benefit and are only used for data governance
All business users should query the warehouse directly without any intermediate layer
questions.explanation: Data marts are subject-specific subsets of the warehouse. A sales data mart might pre-aggregate revenue by region/product and denormalize supporting dimensions, enabling instant response to common sales queries. This topology reduces load on the central warehouse and improves user experience for specific teams.
